item.title: Dream of home ownership becomes reality for 31 families at Nepani
item.description: The dream of owning a home has become a reality for 31 families after they received the keys to their new homes at the Housing Authority's Nepani Subdivision today. While officiating at the handover ceremony, Prime Minister, Sitiveni Rabuka congratulated the new homeowners and encouraged them to build safe and loving homes where their children can grow and thrive. Rabuka acknowledged the role the
item.content:encoded:
The dream of owning a home has become a reality for 31 families after they received the keys to their new homes at the Housing Authority's Nepani Subdivision today. While officiating at the handover ceremony, Prime Minister, Sitiveni Rabuka congratulated the new homeowners and encouraged them to build safe and loving homes where their children can grow and thrive.

Rabuka acknowledged the role the Housing Authority in helping families achieve home ownership, saying it gives them the opportunity to build a better future.

He also commended Minister for Housing and Local Government Maciu Nalumisa for exploring successful affordable housing models overseas that could benefit Fiji.

Rabuka highlighted examples from overseas where surplus building materials are used to build homes for vulnerable people, including former offenders, people recovering from drug addiction and those experiencing homelessness, giving them a fresh start in life. He urged the 31 new homeowners to cherish their homes, support one another and provide a secure environment for their families and future generations.

Rabuka shared his own family's journey to home ownership, recalling how his parents bought a piece of land in Nabua in the late 1950s and spent almost 10 years paying it off before securing a Housing Authority "Borrow and Build" loan of $5,400 to construct their family home. He says the family built the house themselves, mixing concrete, making concrete blocks and laying the foundation by hand, adding that the home still stands today. The Prime Minister also revealed that he later paid off the Housing Authority loan after returning from peacekeeping duties in Lebanon in 1981.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Concerns raised after video appears to show Muslim student singled out at Lautoka school
item.description: Concerns have been raised after a video circulating on social media appeared to show a man preaching at a prominent school in Lautoka, during which a student was singled out in front of classmates because of his faith. In the video, the man, identified online as Osaslugo, is seen addressing students before calling attention to a child he identifies as Muslim.
item.content:encoded:
Concerns have been raised after a video circulating on social media appeared to show a man preaching at a prominent school in Lautoka, during which a student was singled out in front of classmates because of his faith.

In the video, the man, identified online as Osaslugo, is seen addressing students before calling attention to a child he identifies as Muslim.

Osaslugo is a Christian content creator and preacher with a large following across platforms such as TikTok, Instagram and Facebook.

Osaslugo appears to tell the student that because he does not follow Jesus Christ or Christianity, he will not go to heaven.

The footage has sparked criticism online, with many expressing concern that the child was publicly singled out because of his religion and subjected to comments that could be viewed as discriminatory and inappropriate in a school setting.

Questions have been raised about how the preacher was allowed to address students and whether the school had approved the session.

Questions have been sent to the Ministry of Education regarding the incident and whether any action will be taken.

item.title: Taveuni Buses Limited to resume services this afternoon, thanks residents for helping students
item.description: Speaking to fijivillage News, Managing Director Sami Prakash apologised to students, parents and schools for the disruption caused over the past two days. Prakash also thanked the residents and businesses who stepped in to drop students, while school bus services were suspended.
item.content:encoded:
Speaking to fijivillage News, Managing Director Sami Prakash apologised to students, parents and schools for the disruption caused over the past two days.

Prakash also thanked the residents and businesses who stepped in to drop students, while school bus services were suspended.

He says members of the community, including business owners and residents who volunteered their vehicles, played an important role in ensuring many students, particularly those sitting exams, were able to get to and from school during the disruption.

Prakash says the company appreciates the support shown by the community and thanked everyone who assisted while services were suspended.

He assured parents and schools that Taveuni Buses will continue providing safe and reliable transport for students across the island.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Trump signs order imposing 50% tariff on Canadian imports
item.description: US President Donald Trump has signed an executive order imposing a 50 percent tariff on a wide range of goods imported from Canada, escalating trade tensions between the two neighbouring countries. The new tariffs will take effect on August 19th and were introduced in response to what Trump described as "unequal treatment" of American cars, dairy products and alcohol.
item.content:encoded:
US President Donald Trump has signed an executive order imposing a 50 percent tariff on a wide range of goods imported from Canada, escalating trade tensions between the two neighbouring countries.

The new tariffs will take effect on August 19th and were introduced in response to what Trump described as "unequal treatment" of American cars, dairy products and alcohol.

The White House says the measures are aimed at protecting American businesses.

The tariffs will apply to a range of products, including wine, hockey sticks and commercial cement. However, key Canadian exports such as energy, potash, critical minerals and fish have been exempted.

The latest move adds to existing US tariffs of between 15 and 50 percent on Canadian steel, aluminium and copper, as well as a 35 percent tariff on Canadian softwood lumber and a 25 percent tax on non-US parts used in vehicles.

Canada has also imposed 25 percent counter-tariffs on selected American steel, aluminium and vehicle imports.

While Trump recently threatened tariffs over wildfire smoke from Canada drifting into US cities, the executive order instead cites long-standing trade disputes involving the automotive, dairy and alcohol sectors.

The US claims Canada unfairly taxes American vehicles and parts not covered under the US-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA), while also criticising Canada's dairy import system, which applies tariffs of more than 300 percent on imports above set quotas.

Canadian officials have been negotiating with the US in an effort to reduce existing tariffs.

President and CEO of the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, Candace Laing described the decision as "regrettable" but urged both sides to make meaningful progress in negotiations before the new tariffs come into effect.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Man accused of threatening to burn Suva released as investigation continues
item.description: Police say the interview process has been suspended, while investigators await the results of a digital forensic examination of the suspect's mobile phone. Police are reminding people to use social media responsibly, saying freedom of speech and expression come with responsibility.
item.content:encoded:
Police say the interview process has been suspended, while investigators await the results of a digital forensic examination of the suspect's mobile phone.

Police are reminding people to use social media responsibly, saying freedom of speech and expression come with responsibility.

They warn that anyone who makes or shares posts encouraging violence or threatening public safety may be investigated for offences including urging political violence, criminal intimidation and incitement to violence.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Taveuni residents step in to get students to school amid bus disruption
item.description: As students in Taveuni struggled to get to school following the disruption to bus services, two residents stepped in to ensure children, especially those sitting exams, did not miss out on their education. Suncity Supermarket owner Rohil Chaudhary paid for a bus to transport students to and from school yesterday afternoon and again this morning after learning that many children had been left witho
item.content:encoded:
As students in Taveuni struggled to get to school following the disruption to bus services, two residents stepped in to ensure children, especially those sitting exams, did not miss out on their education.

Suncity Supermarket owner Rohil Chaudhary paid for a bus to transport students to and from school yesterday afternoon and again this morning after learning that many children had been left without transport.

Speaking to fijivillage News, Chaudhary says students should not suffer because of a situation they did not create.

He says children are Fiji's future, and it was important to ensure they reached school safely, especially during exam week.

According to Chaudhary, his decision was not about publicity or politics but simply about helping his community in a time of need.

He arranged for Taveuni Buses Limited to transport as many students as possible along the Qeleni to Wairiki route at no cost to the students.

Chaudhary says while the Government had announced contingency plans, he believed the community also had a responsibility to help until normal bus services resumed.

Meanwhile, another South Taveuni resident, Abhishek Sapra, also stepped in by using his own vehicle to transport students to school.

He says the owner of South Taveuni Stores, located near their community, also offered to transport students from Ura to school during the disruption.

Sapra says around 14 students travelled with him , while other community members also helped, allowing the limited minibus services to focus on students travelling from more isolated areas such as Navakuwau, Nakorovou and Kanacea villages.

He says helping students get to school is something many families in the area have always done whenever children are stranded or miss the bus.

He is calling on authorities to act more quickly when essential services are disrupted so that children and other members of the public are not affected.

Earlier this week, several bus operators in Taveuni suspended school bus services over fare-related concerns, leaving many students without transport before temporary arrangements were put in place.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: 48-yr-old woman charged with murder over fatal Samabula flat fire

item.title: Kiran urges youth to champion peace and protect Fiji's unity
item.description: Minister for Women, Children and Social Protection, Sashi Kiran has challenged young people to become champions of peace and socia
